speaker: interviewer
text: --on March 19, 1995. You were talking about Mechelen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we were talking about the raids that they started on Jews. Before I go into the Final Solution for the Jews in Belgium, I have to mention that as the laws against Jews started to be applied to Belgium, what followed the anti-Jewish laws was the establishment of the Gestapo headquarters special section for Jews on one of the main streets in Brussels-- also one of the nicest, and most elegant avenues in Brussels. It was Avenue Louise. That's where they established a headquarters in a huge building, which is-- still today has a commemorative plaque. That was the center-- the headquarters-- for the destruction of the Belgian, the Belgian Jewry. That's where it all came from. They established themselves there. The system was the same all over Europe, as we learned later.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted-- and they did-- establish a Jewish committee, a committee of permanent Jews. That made their work a lot easier because how would they know every Jew in the city? It's only the Jews that knew where the Jews are. So they established-- they got in touch, somehow, with the Jews. And some were prominent. Most of them were not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were people that, in the community, were completely unknown. They were, most of them-- I wouldn't say most of them. Some of them were refugees from Austria, Jewish refugees from Austria or Germany. They had-- very easy to communicate with them because they were speaking their language. These Jews that they picked for the committee-- one of them, the president of the committee, was a certain Blum. He was a Jewish a business man. He was an assimilated Jew. He had nothing to do, really, with any Jewish organization. He was very wealthy and he was a Jew. He was one of the largest textile dealers in Belgium. And Hitler made him president. They also included the chief rabbi. And they promised these people that they will be protected if they work with them and if they do what they tell them to do. I would say that, in my opinion, that some Jewish people that were in that Judenrat were anxious to be there because they saw in this a way of saving their lives and the lives of their families. Even so, I would give them the credit that they did not know, at that time, that they're going to kill the Jews. They thought there'll be restrictions of Jews that they will help enforce. I, I think had they known what that really means and what will come out of that committee, I don't think that these people would have joined the committee. But in any case, they made-- whatever they did, they made the task of the Gestapo easier because they worked with them. The main thing what they did was to supply the Gestapo with all the names and addresses of the Jews in the city. They were very clever, the Germans, how to work. They were working with a plan. They worked scientifically, psychologically. They didn't want the Jews to know what was going to happen to them because they wanted the minimum of resistance. Also, they did want to keep the image. This was Western Europe. It wasn't Poland, and not everybody was an anti-Semite. They wanted to keep the image that they're not murderers in Western Europe. So they would do the dirty work-- take the Jews and do the dirty work in Poland where the population collaborated with them because they were just this anti-Semitic as the Nazis, or worse. But in Western Europe, they had to act in a different way. What they did-- they created this Judenrat. Judenrat, that committee, got together and they asked all the Jews to register with that Judenrat. They have to come, stay in line, and register, fill out the registration-- who they are, where they live, how many people they are, the address, the telephone number, and so forth. If you did not register-- the Germans said if you did not register with the Judenrat, you'd be sent to a concentration camp. You'll be punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the fear was great. And the great majority of Jews were standing in line to register with the Judenrat. A friend of mine-- his father decided not to register with the Judenrat. And they were never caught, and they were never deported. They survived the war because the Gestapo wouldn't know about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But 90-- I don't-- I would say 98% of the Jews, for fear, registered with the Judenrat. No one knew the consequences. And so did we. We registered. Our whole entire family registered. And after we registered, a short while later, the Judenrat sent out like a subpoena. It was a gray paper, which I still have. And it was-- the, the, the letterhead was from the military commander of Belgium in northern France that you are ordered to report to the camp in Mechelen at this and this date. And you have to take along with you food for, for two weeks, and you shouldn't take any food that spoils. And you should take along a pair of working shoes and two pairs of shoes and a sweater and all the things that you would need, and underwear. How much underwear-- they told you what you're allowed to take. Two pair of this, one pair of socks, two pair of underwear, whatever. I still have that paper, which I will be showing to you later. And also, a warning-- if you do not report on that particular day to that camp in Mechelen, you'll be sent to a concentration camp in Germany, which everybody feared because we-- that we did know from the refugees that came from Germany and Austria that to be in a concentration camp in Germany is like being in hell. It's-- it's everything. It's torture. It's no food. It's hell. The stories that we heard made us enough fear that the majority of Jews that received these subpoenas reported to, to, to, to Mechelen. The Judenrat told them this is for work and so did the paper say that this is for work in Germany. And I remember my father went to-- my parents did not get it. In the beginning, they sent it mostly to young people. Later on, they sent it to everybody. My father went to the Judenrat, and he ask-- he asked the rabbi-- was the, the, the wartime rabbi. There was a chief rabbi in Belgium but most rabbis fled. Well, he remained and he-- they made him the chief rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked him if it's for work, why did the children-- six years old, eight years old-- why did they get the subpoena? What kind of work can they do? And he answered, well, the children-- they will water the flowers in the gardens in Germany. I don't know if he was naive or if he-- didn't make sense.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, during the war, I had a teacher. And he, he was teaching us-- a group of boys-- he was teaching us Hebrew and Talmud. He was a refugee himself from Vienna. He was a refugee already from 1935-- 1938. I'm sorry. And he told us, with the Germans, you should have one rule. Whatever they tell you, you should do the opposite, and you'll stay alive. If you do what they tell you to do, you, you won't stay alive. And I remembered what he said. I took it seriously. And my brother and me-- we decided that we're not going. They told us to go, so we're not going. At that time, we decide to liquidate the apartment and to go into hiding and not to report to Mechelen. The great majority of Jews did report. And not only they report, but they went there in their Sunday-- Saturday clothing. They thought they going to a party or something. They were naive. We took the furniture, and we put it in a warehouse. We didn't leave anything in the apartment. And we went into hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you go into hiding? We went to neighborhood where there were no Jews. You know, Brussels a big city. There weren't that many Jews. Jews lived mostly in certain quarters. Like, there were two neighborhoods, or three, that were-- had the Jewish concentration.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a neighborhood where there were no Jews. It was not known as a Jewish neighborhood. And we went to a small-- to a house that was renting furnished rooms. We rang the bell, and we had to take a chance. We told the woman, we are Jews. We want to rent a furnished apartment. We want to hide. And she looked at us and she said, OK. I'll rent you an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take money with you? Did you have enough money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had money. We had-- we had-- we had-- we had a certain amount of money, and that helped because that woman that rented us the apartment, she was not an idealist. She did it for money. We didn't know how, how faithful she'll be, or if she'll report to us to the Germans. But we had no other way. We had to take a chance. And we took that apartment. And we installed our parents, and we installed ourselves in that apartment. And in the meantime, Jews were going every day to Mechelen by the thousands. The transports were going. I want to come back to my sister-in-law that was caught in the-- in the raid. These were the first few hundred Jews that they caught. They-- as I said, they brought these Jews to Breendonk. From Breendonk, they send them to, to Mechelen. And these Jews were on the first transport. They called a transport 1,000 Jews. They accumulated in that camp in Mechelen. Mechelen was a former Belgian military barracks that they confiscated, that they took over and made what they called a Sammellager-- an assembly camp for Jews. They had 1,000 Jews there. They took away everything they had. They took away their identity cards. They gave them a number, which was on a piece of cardboard and a string around the neck. That's what they were. They were a number. And once there was 1,000, they had railroad tracks along one side of the camp. The camp was huge. And they would take the Jews through the front gate, straight to the railroad tracks. There was waiting a cattle train. They loaded them into the trains. They locked the doors once there was 1,000, sealed the doors and shipped them straight to Auschwitz. That ride would have taken a few nights and a few days. Many people, certainly the elderly, would arrive dead. But we went into hiding. And we stayed in that apartment. I don't remember. It was not a very long time because after a while we-- the woman would do the shopping for us. We did not go out in street. The woman had an elderly father, and he would do the shopping for us. And after a few weeks, she came and she said that we have to leave, that this place is too dangerous, that she thinks that they know that there are Jews in this house. In the meantime, while we were there, there was-- there were more Jewish people coming into our house. My brother's father-in-law with two sons didn't have-- he lived in Antwerp. He escaped, and he came to join us into hiding. And she said that we have to leave. And she gave us-- she had another house that she was renting in a different state, not far away. She said we should move to the other house, and we should split. And my brother's father-in-law, with his two sons-- she put them in another house because they said there's too many Jews in one place. And we were quite comfortable in that house. We had a nice apartment. And we had a backyard. So occasionally, in the summer, we would-- when the weather was nice, we were sitting in the backyard so we had fresh air. We noticed in the backyard that a few houses away, we could spot that they were Jewish people. You could recognize Jews in hiding. And we found out they were Jews from Holland because many Jews from Holland were hiding in Belgium. It was extremely difficult to hide in Holland because Holland had a great number of Nazi's-- uniformed Dutch Nazis, SS, and party members. And it was very dangerous to hide in Holland because, as you see in the Anne Frank story, they were denounced and they were informed and then the Nazis arrested them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of Dutch Jews came to hide in Belgium. It was more difficult for them to hide in Belgium because they didn't know the country. And they didn't know the city. They didn't know how to approach. And many of them were caught trying hiding. But many of them did hide in Belgium. So we found out a few houses away, there were Dutch Jews hiding. And that was going on-- we were hiding already a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, we heard that the Dutch Jews were picked up the night before. And a woman came running, and she said we have information that the Gestapo's going to pick you up tonight. And you have to leave immediately. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It wasn't easy to leave immediately, but there's nothing else we could do. We, we couldn't take anything with us. We took ourselves, and we split up. I had one sister that was hiding in an entirely different-- the other end of the city. She was hiding there. So my parents went to her. It was very dangerous to travel by trolley. But it was too far a distance to walk. It was dangerous to walk, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this during the day that they moved, that they traveled?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we waited till the evening because it was more dangerous in the daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you father shave his beard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you try to dress in a nondescript--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. We tried to-- we all tried to look as little Jewish as possible. Of course, we-- at that time, when we went to hiding-- we discarded the yellow star. One of the laws that, that were set up, as I mentioned before-- the laws against the Jews-- one of the laws was that every Jew has to wear a yellow star on the outerwear. If you-- was on a suit, was on the jacket. If it was in the winter, on the coat. And it was not supposed to hide it. That star had to be open. But that law with the yellow star-- the star that, before the Final Solution, went into effect-- that was one of the anti-Jewish laws when they-- when they limited the Jews to walk on the streets. When there was a curfew and they took away the business. That was one of the laws that they made it stricter and stricter on Jews, more difficult to live. Once we went into a hiding, we had to ignore all the anti-Jewish laws. There was no star. There was no-- the identity cards of Jews-- in Belgium, even today, every person has to carry an identity card at all times. Jews had a big stamp, Juif, Jew, on the identity card. So wherever you go and show the identity card, they should know that you are a Jew and treat you accordingly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So once we went to hiding, we didn't have the identity card. We had a false identity card. We had-- I had an identity card as a Belgian, non-Jewish. My name was Jules Moreau. The identity card was fake, but it was real. It was not a forged identity card. It was a card from a small town that the Underground provided us with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were registered in that small town. If the Gestapo would ask if there is a Jules Moreau in that town, they would say yes, there is. We were registered with the-- with the city, with the-- with what they called the Bureau of Population. Anyway, we had-- coming back, we had to leave that hiding place immediately. We waited, I think, till the evening. And my parents went to my older sister. The, the family of my brother-- his father-in-law and the two sons-- they went into a private home of people that were in the Resistance, in the Underground. And we went to my sister, who was hiding also in an entirely different place-- in different neighborhood in the city, a non-Jewish neighborhood. We came to that hiding place of my sister. It was a hotel. It was a small hotel-- not so small because there were a lot of people in there. But it, it wasn't-- it was a cheap hotel. It wasn't a two star hotel or a four star. It was a cheap hotel. There was a cafe downstairs. In Belgium, there's a lot of the cafes. Every block, you have cafes. So downstairs was a cafe. The cafe owner was the owner of the hotel. And my sister asked him if he could give us a room for my brother and myself. And he agreed. He gave us a room. And the next day, when we looked around, we saw it was a very dangerous play to hide-- place to hide because there were dozens of Dutch Jews in that hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the more Jews were concentrated in one place, the more dangerous the place was to be exposed. And we, we told our sister, how can you stay here? You're going to be caught. And we decided that we have to leave that place and that my sister and my brother-in-law have to leave that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a-- through the woman that was renting us the apartment, her son was-- anyway, that's what he claimed, that he was a member of the Underground. I don't really know if he was. I don't know. I never trusted that boy. But he did have a contract with the underground because we were contacted by another young man. His name was Piere Carnavelle. And that young man came to see us. And I won't say that this man was an angel. He wasn't a man. He was a very wealthy boy. He was-- he was like 20, 20 or 21 years old. His parents were extremely wealthy. They were from nobility. His father was British and escaped to England when the war broke out. His mother was Belgian. And through him-- through this Piere Carnavelle, we didn't have where to hide, and he provided-- he provided a hiding place for us and for my brother's family and for my sister.
